description: This line chart presents Scopus bibliometric statistical data of annual publication counts for two steel-related research topics from 2010 to 2022. The x-axis represents the search year, and the y-axis denotes the number of search results in thousands. The blue line corresponds to publications on general light-weight steel, while the green line corresponds to publications on Fe-Mn-Al-C steel, a specific type of light-weight steel. Both curves show a continuous, steady increasing trend across the entire studied period: publications on light-weight steel grow from ~13 thousand in 2010 to ~48 thousand in 2022, while publications on Fe-Mn-Al-C steel grow from ~8 thousand in 2010 to ~30 thousand in 2022. Publication volume for general light-weight steel is consistently higher than that for Fe-Mn-Al-C steel across all years. This figure confirms the rapidly growing global research attention on light-weight steel systems, including the specific Fe-Mn-Al-C steel category, supporting the relevance of further research on these material systems for low-density structural applications.

This flow chart classifies Fe-Mn-Al-C steels into two main categories based on chemical composition, summarizes their mechanical property balance, and lists corresponding application areas. The first category is multiphase Fe-Mn-Al-C steels with 5-12 wt.% Mn and ≤ 0.8 wt.% C, which exhibits a strength range of 800–1200 MPa and elongation of 30–60%, and is applied in automotive structural components and reinforcements. The second category is austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steels with >12 wt.% Mn and 0.8-1.2 wt.% C, which shows a wider strength range of 600–1500 MPa and elongation lower than 60%, and is suitable for automotive structural components as well as military fields. The figure demonstrates that both types of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels possess favorable strength-ductility balance, supporting their great application potential in lightweight structural scenarios.

This figure presents two calculated isothermal phase diagrams of the Fe-20Mn-Al-C lightweight steel system. Subplot (a) illustrates equilibrium phase regions at 900 °C, while subplot (b) shows phase regions at 1200 °C. Both diagrams use weight percent Al (0-20 wt%) as the x-axis, and weight percent C (0-5.0 wt%) as the y-axis. Identified phases include austenite (γ), ferrite (α), κ-carbide, M₃C, Al₄C₃, graphite (gra.), and liquid phase (L, only present in the 1200 °C diagram). Experimental data points marked by different symbols are overlaid, matching well with calculated phase regions. Obvious temperature-dependent phase region shifts are observed: liquid-containing regions (L, γ+L) exist at 1200 °C, while M₃C + graphite stable regions appear at 900 °C. This figure validates thermodynamic calculation results for the alloy system, supporting their application for composition design and heat treatment optimization of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels.

This figure contains two scatter plots showing room-temperature mechanical properties of lightweight Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, with subplot (a) for medium Mn content (8-12 wt%) and subplot (b) for high Mn content (12-40 wt%). The X-axis represents elongation (%), while the Y-axis represents yield strength (YS) and ultimate tensile strength (UTS, MPa). Different markers correspond to varied C content (0.5 wt% to >1.2 wt%) and different Mn content ranges. Marked microstructure types include γ-based duplex (γ+α/δ) and single-γ. High Mn steels achieve much higher maximum elongation (~100%) with UTS up to 2000 MPa, while medium Mn steels have maximum elongation around 50% and maximum UTS around 1400 MPa. The results demonstrate that Mn and C concentrations significantly regulate the phase composition and mechanical performance of lightweight steels, enabling tunable strength-ductility combinations for different application demands.

This figure illustrates the effect of Si content (0, 1, 2 mass%) on the microstructure and tensile mechanical properties of homogenized Fe-30Mn-9Al-1.2C lightweight austenitic steels. The first subfigure is a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrograph with a 50 nm scale bar, showing uniformly distributed nanoscale precipitates in the matrix. The second subfigure is energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping with a 20 nm scale bar, marking distributions of Fe, Al, Si, C to verify C-rich κ-carbide precipitates. The third subfigure presents engineering stress-strain curves, with x-axis as engineering strain (%) and y-axis as engineering stress (MPa): the black curve corresponds to 0Si steel, red to 1Si, blue to 2Si. It shows that increasing Si content significantly enhances strength: the 2Si sample has a yield strength of ~950 MPa, while the 0Si sample has the highest elongation (~75%), 1Si has ~55% elongation, 2Si has ~20% elongation. The last subfigure is another nanoscale elemental distribution map with a 25 nm scale bar, showing element segregation associated with κ-carbides. The core conclusion is that Si addition promotes the formation of intragranular κ-carbides, leading to remarkable strengthening of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, which matches thermodynamic calculation predictions.

This set of 4 SEM-EBSD phase maps displays the microstructural phase distribution of Fe–20Mn–9Al–3Cr-xC low-density steel strips with varied carbon content: subfigure (a) corresponds to 0.8 wt% C, (b) to 1.0 wt% C, (c) to 1.2 wt% C, (d) to 1.4 wt% C, with a unified 50 μm scale bar for all subfigures. Red contrast denotes ferrite phase, and gray contrast denotes austenite phase. A distinct trend is observed: as C content increases from 0.8 wt% to 1.4 wt%, the volume fraction of ferrite reduces remarkably from 36 vol% to 4 vol%, while austenite becomes the dominant matrix phase. This figure supports the core conclusion that carbon content effectively tunes the phase constitution of lightweight Fe-Mn-Al-C steels: elevated C content stabilizes austenite, facilitates κ-carbide precipitation, and increases the steel yield strength from 680 MPa to 992 MPa while retaining elongation ≥ 33%.

This figure presents electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) results of Fe–28Mn–9Al–0.8C–3Cu lightweight steel strips, with a 2 μm scale bar for all micrographs and a color scale ranging from 3.500 to 8.000 for elemental distribution intensity. Subplots (a-d) show secondary electron microstructure morphologies, while (e-h) show corresponding elemental distribution maps: (a,e) correspond to the as-cast sample, (b,f) to the sample annealed at 500 °C, (c,g) to the sample annealed at 600 °C, and (d,h) to the sample annealed at 700 °C. It is observed that with increasing annealing temperature, the size and number of large Cu-rich regions gradually decrease, while the count of fine Cu-rich precipitates in the austenite matrix increases. The results validate that 3 wt% Cu addition suppresses intergranular κ-carbide formation during 500–700 °C annealing, leading to improved strength with a slight reduction in total elongation, supporting that Cu alloying combined with annealing can effectively optimize the mechanical performance of Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels.

This figure is a temperature-time process diagram illustrating four distinct thermomechanical processing routes for Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels, with the x-axis representing time and the y-axis representing temperature. The shared pre-processing steps include solution treatment at 1100-1200 °C, followed by hot rolling, cooling, and cold rolling. Four post-cold-rolling routes are designed: Route 1 applies direct cooling after cold rolling without high-temperature annealing; Route 2 adopts annealing at 1100-1200 °C followed by direct cooling; Route 3 uses 1100-1200 °C annealing then cooling to 450-700 °C for aging; Route 4 performs prolonged aging at 450-700 °C after annealing. These routes are developed to regulate the microstructure (including κ-carbide precipitation, grain structure, phase composition) and final mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, supporting process optimization for their industrial manufacturing.
